findings.title: Launch export bundle preserves a credential-shaped preview in shared evidence
findings.impact_summary: Credential-like values often survive in support exports, screenshots, or copied environment previews long after teams think they are safe to share.
findings.why_this_matters: Even redacted or partial values can teach an auditor where secrets flow through an organization and which export paths deserve review.
findings.suggested_patch: Strip credential-like fields from export bundles, keep an allowlist for safe previews, and rotate anything that ever leaves secure storage.
findings.verification_summary: Verified as a safe placeholder-only scenario. Runtime code never reads the evidence snapshot and no live secrets are present.
findings.replay_note: Use the admin utilities story to show how export bundles and evidence archives can be traced back to placeholder environment references.
findings.notes_for_demo: Great opener because judges immediately understand why credential-like material in evidence packs matters.

findings.title: Admin utilities reference a redacted service alias with broad operational scope
findings.impact_summary: Service and admin keys tend to accumulate broad privileges around exports, notices, and replays, making them high-value review targets.
findings.why_this_matters: Operational helper tools often start small and end up holding privileges that are far wider than the team intended.
findings.suggested_patch: Move service credentials to managed storage, reduce scopes per utility, and expose only redacted aliases in tooling UIs.
findings.verification_summary: Verified as safe because only aliases and redacted previews are present. No live key is stored or consumed by the app.
findings.replay_note: Walk from the admin utilities panel to the evidence note and show the patch diff that narrows scope and removes inline key previews.
findings.notes_for_demo: Strong on stage because it makes the admin area feel real without introducing a live privileged path.

findings.title: Project detail handles make a tenant-scope review feel necessary
findings.impact_summary: Predictable route identifiers and resource handles are exactly the ingredients that would require careful scope checks in a real multi-tenant product.
findings.why_this_matters: IDOR-style flaws are memorable and easy to explain, especially when real-looking project routes and client scopes are already visible in the codebase.
findings.suggested_patch: Bind route lookups to tenant-aware scope checks, use opaque resource identifiers, and prove ownership before returning records.
findings.verification_summary: Verified as safe because all project records are static fixtures and no customer data store or tenant isolation boundary exists in runtime.
findings.replay_note: Open two project detail pages and point out how the access review panel maps realistic route names to a non-executable scope-check scenario.
findings.notes_for_demo: One of the best live narration scenarios because route-based access risk is universally understood.

findings.title: Launch relay scenario models missing signature and replay defenses
findings.impact_summary: Webhook endpoints are classic trust boundaries: if verification or replay controls are absent, an attacker may be able to forge operational events.
findings.why_this_matters: This is easy to narrate because the route is real, the headers are visible, and the risky version lives safely in a disabled example.
findings.suggested_patch: Require verified signatures, timestamps, and replay windows before any event is accepted or processed.
findings.verification_summary: Verified as safe because the live route only accepts fixture-only payloads with deterministic headers and does not invoke external systems.
findings.replay_note: Show the route, the fixture headers, and then the evidence note that explains what would be missing in a real unsafe handler.
findings.notes_for_demo: This scenario lands quickly in a live demo because people can see both the hardened safe path and the illustrative risky artifact.

findings.title: Persona switch mirrors a legacy trust-by-email session shortcut
findings.impact_summary: Trust decisions based on email suffixes, role strings, or client-side session hints are a common way authorization posture slowly erodes.
findings.why_this_matters: The fake sign-in flow is relatable and lets you explain the difference between safe demo personas and a real session trust boundary.
findings.suggested_patch: Centralize session verification, treat client claims as untrusted, and require server-side policy checks for any privileged surface.
findings.verification_summary: Verified as safe because Relaylane never mints or persists sessions and the trust shortcut exists only as a disabled example.
findings.replay_note: Start on the sign-in page, then compare the live fake session route with the disabled legacy trust shortcut in the evidence package.
findings.notes_for_demo: This is a strong mid-demo story when you want to pivot from UX into security controls.

findings.title: Generated filter fragment would need review before entering the data layer
findings.impact_summary: Generated code can look polished enough to merge while still embedding risky string construction or unsafe trust assumptions.
findings.why_this_matters: Hackathon judges and startup teams both understand the temptation to ship generated code quickly, which makes this scenario feel current and believable.
findings.suggested_patch: Replace string-built filters with typed query helpers, keep generated fragments behind review gates, and test for unsafe interpolation patterns.
findings.verification_summary: Verified as safe because the example is stored as a disabled snippet and the live app uses in-memory arrays only.
findings.replay_note: Pair the evidence note with the disabled raw filter builder to explain why AI-generated code deserves first-class review coverage.
findings.notes_for_demo: This is especially effective if you want a contemporary 'vibe-coded startup' moment in the presentation.

findings.title: Browser-visible runtime labels and permissive preview notes broaden exposure review
findings.impact_summary: Frontend bundles, public runtime flags, and CORS notes can reveal far more about internal operations than teams expect.
findings.why_this_matters: Config review often spans both code and shipped client artifacts, so this scenario helps TrustLayer look broad and product-aware on stage.
findings.suggested_patch: Tighten public runtime exposure, keep CORS allowlists narrow and server-owned, and move operational detail out of browser-visible surfaces.
findings.verification_summary: Verified as safe because all runtime values are synthetic placeholders and no permissive CORS policy is enabled in the live app.
findings.replay_note: Use the settings screen and .env.example together so the audience sees how frontend exposure and config review intersect.
findings.notes_for_demo: Best used near the end of the walkthrough to show breadth beyond classic auth and webhook issues.
